problem_addressed: Fairness audits require large labeled datasets which are costly and labor-intensive to obtain in biomedical applications.
core_claim: Infairness framework enables reliable fairness auditing with limited labeled data by combining small labeled datasets with large unlabeled datasets through semi-supervised inference.
key_contributions: Introduction of Infairness unified framework for auditing multiple fairness criteria using semi-supervised inference
Demonstration of robustness to ML and imputation model specifications
Empirical validation showing 50% variance reduction compared to supervised estimation in real-world audits
novelty: Semi-supervised approach that imputes missing outcomes via regression with carefully selected nonlinear basis functions, allowing leverage of unlabeled data while maintaining robustness to model misspecification.
method_summary: Combines small labeled dataset with large unlabeled dataset by imputing missing outcomes via regression with nonlinear basis functions, then evaluates fairness criteria on the completed dataset.
method_components: Labeled data processing for initial parameter estimation
Unlabeled data imputation using regression with nonlinear basis functions
Fairness criterion evaluation on imputed dataset
key_innovation: Leveraging auxiliary covariates (W) through carefully specified imputation models to improve efficiency while maintaining robustness to model misspecification.
assumptions: Availability of auxiliary covariates W for imputation
Appropriate selection of nonlinear basis functions for imputation model
Fairness criteria can be expressed as functions of model predictions and ground truth outcomes
theoretical_grounding: Extensive theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrating robustness to model specification and substantial efficiency gains over supervised estimation through simulation studies and real-world applications.
hyperparameters: K=3 equally spaced knots for spline basis; bandwidth h_a = σ_a / n_a^{-0.45} for NP; BIC-based model selection for Infairness with penalty -2ℓ(θ) + d_a * min{n_a^{0.1}, log(n_a)}
headline_result: Infairness achieves lowest bias and variance for ∆TPR estimation on MIMIC-III
improvement_over_baseline: Infairness reduces ∆TPR bias by 87.5% compared to SUP (0.02 vs 0.15)
stated_limitations: Limited to fairness auditing applications rather than general semi-supervised learning
May not address all types of fairness criteria comprehensively
Assumes availability of unlabeled data which might not always be accessible
unstated_weaknesses: Does not directly address label noise, which is central to the research project
May not be suitable for all types of medical image data
The imputation method might struggle with highly imbalanced subpopulations
Theoretical analysis might not cover all practical scenarios
threats_to_validity: Generalization risk: The approach may not generalize well to different medical domains or imaging modalities
Confounding factors: The imputation process might introduce biases if not carefully designed
Sample selection bias: The labeled data might not be representative of the overall population
Model misspecification: The robustness claims might not hold for all types of ML models
open_questions: How does the approach perform under different levels and types of label noise?
Can the method be adapted for image-based fairness auditing specifically?
How does the imputation quality affect the fairness audit results?
What are the computational requirements for large-scale medical image datasets?
future_work: Extending the framework to image-based fairness auditing
Incorporating uncertainty quantification in the imputation process
Developing methods for handling label noise in semi-supervised settings
gaps_this_enables: Opens a gap for uncertainty-aware methods in semi-supervised fairness auditing
Enables research on combining fairness auditing with label noise handling
Provides a foundation for developing more robust semi-supervised methods in medical applications
relevance_to_topic: The paper is relevant as it addresses semi-supervised inference in medical applications, though it focuses on fairness auditing rather than label noise handling specifically.
best_result: Infairness achieves lowest bias and variance for ∆TPR estimation on MIMIC-III
limitations_stated: Limited to fairness auditing applications rather than general semi-supervised learning
May not address all types of fairness criteria comprehensively
Assumes availability of unlabeled data which might not always be accessible
title: Reliable fairness auditing with semi-supervised inference
